flashcards.front: What is a collision in hash tables?
flashcards.back: A collision occurs when two different keys produce the same hash value/index. Collisions are inevitable due to the pigeonhole principle (compressing infinite keys into finite slots). Efficient collision resolution maintains O(1) average-case lookup time.

Each table slot maintains a linked list. On collision, elements are inserted at the head. Average search: O(1 + α) where α = n/m (load factor). Pseudocode: INSERT: head = table[h(key)], newNode.next = head, table[h(key)] = newNode. SEARCH: traverse list at h(key). DELETE: remove node from list.

Open addressing method: probe next sequential slot until empty found. Formula: h(k,i) = (h'(k) + i) mod m for i-th probe. Suffers from primary clustering (long runs of occupied slots), degrading performance. Works well with low load factors (< 0.5). Simple to implement.

Uses quadratic increment: h(k,i) = (h'(k) + c₁i + c₂i²) mod m (commonly c₁=0, c₂=1). Reduces primary clustering but causes secondary clustering (keys sharing same initial position follow same path). With prime table size m and c₂≠0, can probe all slots if load factor < 0.5.

Uses two hash functions: h(k,i) = (h₁(k) + i·h₂(k)) mod m. Typically h₁(k)=k mod m, h₂(k)=prime - (k mod prime). Step size varies per key, eliminating both primary and secondary clustering. Requires m to be prime for guaranteed probing of all slots.

Rehashing dynamically resizes the hash table when load factor exceeds threshold (commonly 0.7). Creates new table (~2x size), recomputes all existing keys with new hash function, and discards old table. While costing O(n) temporarily, it reduces collisions and restores O(1) average performance.

Uses two hash tables T1 and T2 with functions h1, h2. Each key can only be at T1[h1(k)] or T2[h2(k)]. Insertion alternates between positions, displacing existing keys. If displacement cycle occurs, rehashing with new functions resolves it. Provides O(1) worst-case lookup and deletion, but load factor limited to ~50%.

Separate Chaining: handles high load, uses extra memory, simple. Open Addressing: cache-friendly, no pointer overhead. Linear Probing: best cache, but clustering. Quadratic/Double: better distribution. Cuckoo Hashing: O(1) worst-case but low load factor. Choose based on α, memory constraints, and consistency requirements.
